content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Please recommend a movie that is GENUINELY terrifying?

**Score:** 32
**Body:** Hey everyone! I’m on the hunt for a horror movie that will actually give me nightmares or make me want to sleep with the lights on. I watch a lot of horror, but lately, nothing seems to scare me anymore.

To give you an idea of my taste: 'The Conjuring' and 'Ghostland' are two movies that genuinely terrified me and gave me that deep, unsettling feeling of pure dread. On the other hand, I’m not a big fan of cheap jump scares or pure gore without a good story—I want something atmosphere-heavy, psychological, or paranormal that gets under your skin.

What is the one movie that absolutely broke you or made you cover your eyes? I'm open to anything (found footage, supernatural, indie gems). Hit me with your absolute scariest picks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t get scared from scary movies much but Talk to Me in movie theaters had me clenched all over.  Pay attention. Sound up. Lights off 
2. Aterrados (Terrified). Same director that did When Evil Lurks. Genuinely chilled me to the bone.
3. The Hills Have Eyes. It just seems very plausible and therefore terrifying. 
4. I remember Contagion being really stressful even before COVID. Good movie from what I remember, I'm just too afraid to rewatch it.
  - Contagion came out about 10 years before Covid. Every time I watch it, I’m amazed by how spot-on it is to the pandemic.
5. Creep terrified me.
6. Hereditary messed me up for weeks, that movie doesn't let you breathe.
7. The Mist. You will NOT forget the ending 
8. Barbarian is such a good watch and just when you think you know whats going on BOOM massive switch. 
9. The Descent.

## 2. Recommend something like Coherence and Triangle.

**Score:** 23
**Body:** Mind bending plot that makes you watch the movie multiple times and still not get what's going on. Preferably following a group of characters rather than a single character.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Primer.
2. The Endless is right up that alley
  - And it's companion/prequels 'Resolution', and to a lesser extent 'spring'.    You'll rewatch Endless and Resolution multiple times
3. Memento
4. **The One I Love (2014)** with Mark Duplass and Elisabeth Moss.

This is an underrated gem.

It's a romance slash drama about a married couple struggling to get over one of them cheating, but there is a huge ***Sci-Fi twist*** that flips the movie on its head about 20-minutes in.

Definitely worth checking out.
5. * Timecrimes
* Time Lapse
6. Predestination 
7. Caddo Lake
8. [Myth of Man](https://reelgood.com/movie/myth-of-man-2025) - One of my two favorite films of all time, it’s a masterpiece of visual storytelling.  It follows a young woman in a dangerous steampunk world, who receives a message from her creator that leads her on an epic journey.  It massively over-performs its minuscule budget.  My buddy thought it had a budget 20 times its actual budget.  Be aware that Winans doesn't spell everything out for you and you will have to think about the film to get some of the details.  Jamin Winan's prior film, [The Frame](https://reelgood.com/movie/the-frame-2014), is also excellent in these regards.

[The Quiet Earth](https://reelgood.com/movie/the-quiet-earth-1985) - My other favorite film.  *Do not read any other reviews or descriptions or watch any previews!!!* They all give away major plot points. Even Reelgood and IMDB give away important information. All you need to know is that it is about a man who wakes up to find that everyone else has disappeared and that it was filmed and set in New Zealand in the mid-'80s. It is a phenomenal slow burn sf movie.
9. # Coherence  is so freakin good, love that movie

## 3. Movies that centers deaf ppl and deaf culture

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Need more movies about deaf ppl and their culture in my life but it’s so few and the couple I’ve  seen absolutely moved me! But that was yrs ago, need a new obsession. I’ve already seen:

Mr. Holland’s opus

CODA

Children of a lesser god (fav. 100000/10. F\*ck u James hunt)

Shape of water 

Also watched switched at birth a few times. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sound of Metal (which I just commented about in another post)
2. Marlee Matlin is an incredible actress who happens to be deaf so I’d highly recommend her movies. 
  - That’s auntie 
  - Not a movie, but she plays a character in The Magicians, and in one episode (S3E8) they have an extended sagement from her POV, where the audio is silent/muffled to represent what her character would be experiencing. Not sure if it’s worth watching without the context of the entire show, but it’s an amazing episode.
3. Side character, but The Family Stone 
4. Beyond Silence (1996)

Hush (2016)
5. Drive My Car has a very lovely sideplot involving a deaf actress.
6. The Tribe (ukrainian film)
7. Baby Driver!
8. How you got through all those movies without having heard of Sound of Metal is beyond me!! I'm definitely due for a rewatch 

## 4. "Coming of age" but for adults who are coming into their own in their 20s?

**Score:** 17
**Body:**  I just graduated university with a degree in Writing/Visual studies. It was a large part of my life and obviously I have a lot of mixed emotions right now. I'm scared for the future (arts degrees aren't known for their employability afterall) but also excited to see how my life changes and grows in the next few months.

I watched Eighth Grade by Bo Burnham shortly after going into highschool and it nailed a lot of the feelings I had back then. I'd like to watch a movie which is about young adults who have just finished university or are going out into the real world. I'm fine with happy or sad (no doubt I've got lots of both) but would like something that is entertaining and with good emotion and dialogue.

Other movies I have enjoyed that have somewhat similar vibes are Adventureland, Her, Columbus, Detachment and Juno (most of these are very sad... whoops) but these are almost all based around high school.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Worst Person In The World would be perfect for this
2. Shiva Baby and Cha Cha Real Smooth
3. Fances Ha
4. St Elmo’s Fire 
5. Garden State
6. Circle of Friends 1995

Set in Ireland in the 1950s so may not be completley relatable, but a brilliant story :-) 
7. Beautiful Girls
8. The Graduate  1967
9. Reality Bites


10. Party Girl - Parker Posey at her best! It's perfect for your situation. 

## 5. Need movie recommendations about overcoming loneliness for my sister who is Deaf and recently went through a breakup with a boyfriend

**Score:** 11
**Body:** My sister was born Deaf and has been unlucky in love at times over the years. She recently went through a bad breakup. Some of her closest friends are living in other cities and or ones near her are busy with kids and marriages. For myself, I sometimes find movies to be healing. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. CODA has a bunch of characters who are deaf and the main character of The Sound of Metal is a person having to deal with becoming deaf. I believe both movies came out around the same time and both did a great job of addressing the kinds of isolation that the condition creates (TSOM even sort of suggests a type of peace that comes from acceptance of it too). Anyways both are great and I recommend both. 
2. The green ray (1986 European movie),

The way (2010),

Three colours :Blue (1993), 

God's own country  (2017),

Amelie (2001),

Lost in Translation (2003),

The martian ,

The unwinding,

The grand highway (1987) 


3. Little Miss Sunshine 
4. Watch Dr Who
5. See if she's ever seen "Children Of A Lesser God",or "Goya's Ghosts".
6. Swingers

High Fidelity 

Bridesmaids 

So I Married an Axe Murder

Sisterhood of the Travelling Pants

Bend it like Beckham
7. \[mid 50's\] Tell her she is not alone. I tried my best in life to find love. Two Marriages, Three finances and several serious girlfriends in between most of them lasting 6 months to a year. First marriage at 18 was over in less that two years. don't ever do that kids. The second marriage lasted 16 years and then I get hit with I don't think I love you anymore, I don't know if I ever did. I'm moving out on Friday. 

Have absolutely zero interest in any girl ever again now but at least I got a cool son out of the deal that still lives with me. 

Don't watch sad movies. Watch something like The Fifth Element. It's a classic. 
8. THE SHAPE OF WATER will be a 10/10 for her I swear give it a try 🎀🫶🏻
9. DTF St Louis
10. Deaf President Now! (2025)

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Never truly understood the aliens from signs

**Score:** 2427
**Body:** So like, they invade a planet, a casual thing you expect from aliens... but later in the film you see their main weakness is water, Earth is 70% water. Plus it rains. And yet these guys come to the planet Butt ass naked. No gear that can protect them.

&#x200B;

And here's an iconic line from the nostalgia critic

&#x200B;

"The only thing that can kill the aliens is water, yeah, it's like acid to them, one drop hurts them like crazy. Hmm... probably should've thought that through before attacking a planet MOSTLY COVERED IN WATER!!!!"

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My favorite part is that Signs and Hook had the same twist of yelling at a child to play baseball more good.
2. To be fair, this still makes more sense than any single scene in The Happening.
  - I thought the premise was interesting when I first heard of it, and then also realized Dale Gribble came up with this plot 8 years earlier.

https://preview.redd.it/gu1zf679hl6h1.jpeg?width=640&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=29de70d610cd64effa1cdaedde362b42cbca2e4e
  - What? No...

![gif](giphy|zjQrmdlR9ZCM)
3. It’s behind!
  - Move children! Vamanos!
4. I've seen notions that the aliens on the planet are there as punishment, with earth serving as a penal colony or a death sentence. That's why they're sent down naked, without any equipment, into a hostile environment.

I've also seen that the figures are described as aliens, but are actually demons, given how much of the film is about faith.

They're just head canons though. Ultimately it doesn't have to make sense. The movie has some of the eeriest fucking scares, without having to resort to jump scares, gore or any of the usual tropes. 
  - Man, the scene where the little girl gets water in the middle of the night and sees one on the roof is masterful. One of the only movies where I remember the jump scares fondly.
    - Their's a monster outside my window, can I have a glass of water?
  - The movie does a good job keeping things vague so we can speculate things like this. We are given a very narrow view of the invasion. 

I always liked the idea that the aliens are completely desperate, they needed Earth's resources a lot more then we knew. They might be flat out starving at this point, enough to risk a trip to the acid planet.

## 2. The opening of Dunkirk has got to be one of the intense openings for a movie

**Score:** 734
**Body:** I remembered seeing this movie in IMAX, and the gunshots from the opening scared the crap out of me since it’s super loud.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 28 weeks later had amazing and intense opening. 
2. For war movie intros, I'd pick Saving Private Ryan as top.
  - ACHYALLY, the opening scene for ryan is the cemetery...
    - This guy Ryans
      - But he doesn't save 
  - Saving Private Ryan, dare I say it, had some optimism that it would get better

The sense of foreboding in Dunkirk just made it altogether a bit more depressing
3. And only mention of French that hold line for evacuation 
4. I was disappointed because Dunkirk looked pristine and empty. Historically, it should have looked heavily bombed and bursting at the seams with soldiers. I believe that Nolan's reluctancy to use CGI hurt this film. And Oppenheimer too, for that matter.

Most intense intro would be SPR for me. I still enjoyed Dunkirk a lot. 
  - Yeah it looked cheap. Like a made for TV movie.

The cinematography was amazing but all it showed was washed out tones on empty beaches.

Atonement does a really good job showing the chaos on the beach in Dunkirk and it uses one long scene to do it as well.


5. i always found it very interesting that you never see any german soldiers in this movie - or at least that's how i perceived the movie

## 3. Steven Spielberg Says James Bond Movies ‘Constantly Turned Me Down’ and ‘If They Asked Me Now’ to Direct the Answer Would Be: ‘You Can’t Afford Me’

**Score:** 719

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I mean he's right. Also Spielberg of 1984 would have been amazing. Spielberg today I think would make an awful Bond film series. 
  - who can create perfect new Bond movie, then?
    - Denis Villeneuve. Hence why he was chosen 
    - Edgar Wright
      - I’m not sure if he could pull it off but I’d love to watch him try 
2. What’s with all the negativity towards Spielberg here? 
3. i like Spielberg and still think his recent movies are good unlike some comments here, but this is such an odd thing to say randomly in a podcast about Disclosure Day

Like... i just can't see them asking Spielberg to direct Bond movies, and saying the studio can't afford him seems like a narcissistic thing to say considering the studio managed to pick Denis Villeneuve (probably one of the most hottest director right now) as the director for the next Bond
  - Depending on the tone of the podcast it’s possible you’re taking this far more seriously than it was said
4. Playing the reverse psychology trick are we.
5. Goddamn the amount of people in here salty over a living legend with MULTIPLE classic, blockbuster films under his belt, told a little story about “the one that got away” is crazy. Peak Reddit behavior to sniff at Spielberg like he’s just some asshole. lol

## 4. What's the most shockingly violent or disturbing scene in an otherwise "normal" or family friendly movie?

**Score:** 639
**Body:** What's the most violent or disturbing scene you've seen in a movie that's otherwise family friendly or pretty normal?

The movie Faith Like Potatoes. Some bland Christian movie I remember watching in church years ago. I barely remember anything except for one moment...

It has a really fucked up scene where a character gets brutally run over and mangled by a tractor and keeps vomiting up a LOT of blood when the driver tries to revive him. It comes out of nowhere too, in an otherwise nothingburger movie. My whole audience was like...what the fuck is this shit? lol

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm never going to see this movie but god what a title.
  - I thought this was like a joke movie or something lol
    - This sounds like a 30 Rock movie, what
2. https://preview.redd.it/ugkq9lwxko6h1.jpeg?width=295&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=6a18a28bdb253df996c6e85a123bab0ce4865cfd

Jesus I made this in 2010, thank you OP for reminding me
3. ![gif](giphy|zVxeZHKKemqWs)


  - This is actually how Paul Rudd looks so young. Every year he sacrifices a child to the Old One living at the bottom of that lake, in exchange for eternal youth.
4. ![gif](giphy|fBPHsZ3CYolmRpKNYY)
5. Some boring forklift safety film I had to watch 30 yrs ago.  Dude left his forks up at eye height and some lady walks around the corner and gets forks to the eyes and screams.   I still think about it obviously lol
6. I remember in 7th grade our teacher had us watch On Golden Pond. When dude goes flying after the boat crash everyone lost their shit. It still makes me laugh thinking of that scene. 
7. Who Framed Roger Rabbit. the shoe in the acid

## 5. Which actor, known for playing protagonists, surprised you with an outstanding villain performance? My pick is Tom Cruise in Collateral.

**Score:** 574
**Body:** Tom Cruise was phenomenal in Collateral. He brought effortless charisma and chilling intensity to the role of Vincent.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Robin Williams in One Hour Photo
  - And insomnia,underrated villain actor lol.
2. My pick is also Tom Cruise:

https://preview.redd.it/1huvek98to6h1.jpeg?width=1600&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=87045804d7c144b3f179526235e3b1c5c6727e19
3. ![gif](giphy|rNgT8P8pL3dn2)
4. Henry Fonda in Once Upon a Time in the West
5. Albert Brooks in Drive
6. Cruise is so awesome in Collateral. Another that comes to mind is Matt Damon in Interstellar. He couldn't just say, "This place isn't good let's gtf out." No, he wants to strand their asses there and steal their ship. Crazytown.
7. Bryan Cranston. Malcolm in the middle to Breaking Bad just blows my mind. Probably one of the best actors of the last 50 or so years.

https://preview.redd.it/17cyip8gso6h1.png?width=168&format=png&auto=webp&s=05ca23efcb8701b22d041303b1819402e379021b
8. In a double turn, ***Platoon.***

Usual good guy Tom Berenger as the evil Barnes

Usual bad guy Willem Dafoe as the heroic Elias
9. Leonardo DiCaprio in Django Unchained

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Deeply personal documentaries that stay with you

**Score:** 38
**Body:** **EDIT: You guys are AMAZING! I created a whole new watchlist just from this post. I will try to watch some of them and maybe I will come back with a new post ❤️**



I have this obsession with these types of documentaries. You know, when the director follows a person or a group of people for years as they grow older and face life changes. In some cases the director becomes part of the story, or does voiceovers. What is it about them?

They basically feel a lot like a movie, because the director starts filming and as time goes by stuff happens and become part of the documentary and by the end of it there is a complete story with an arc that nobody directed, it just happend naturally and a director recordered it so we can all see. These stories touch me deeply, more than documentaries that tell a story that has already happened through interviews and narration.

Am I weird? Is there anybody out there who loves this type of art?

Examples:

**Stevie (2002)**: In 1995, director Steve James (of 'Hoop Dreams') returned to rural Southern Illinois to reconnect with Stevie Fielding, a troubled young boy to whom he had been an "Advocate Big Brother" ten years earlier.

**Hoop Dreams (1994)**: A film following the lives of two inner-city Chicago boys who struggle to become college basketball players on the road to going professional.

**HEROINOHIO (2020)**: Chronicles the transformative efforts of twin brothers Mike and Chuck Rollins through their nonprofit, Gemini Reliance. Over four years, amidst the peak of the opioid crisis, the documentary captures their mission to turn neglected properties into safe, sober living environments for individuals in recovery. While their efforts offer hope and healing, their journey to sobriety remains a continuous and challenging battle.

**American Street Kid (2020)**: Filmmaker, Michael Leoni heads to the streets of LA to shine a light on the epidemic of homeless youth in America. Once inside their world he realizes he can no longer be an observer; every day is a matter of life or death and he'll do anything to get them off the streets.

**Streetwise (1984)**: Gritty documentary that looks at the lives of teenagers living on the streets of Seattle.

**Children Underground (2001)**: A profile of homeless Romanian children who were born victims of the nation's reckless population growth policy during its communist era.

**Agelastos Petra (2000)**: The past and the present coexist in a place spoiled by modern industry but which long ago hosted the Eleusinian Mysteries, the secret ceremonies that initiated the ancient Greeks into the miracles of life, death and the afterlife.

**Bombay Beach (2011)**: Bombay Beach is one of the poorest communities in southern California located on the shores of the Salton Sea, a man-made sea stranded in the middle of the Colorado desert that was once a beautiful vacation destination for the privileged and is now a pool of dead fish. Film director Alma Har'el tells the story of three protagonists. The trials of Benny Parrish, a young boy diagnosed with bipolar disorder whose troubled soul and vivid imagination create both suffering and joy for him and his complex and loving family. The story of CeeJay Thompson, a black teenager and aspiring football player who has taken refuge in Bombay Beach hoping to avoid the same fate of his cousin who was murdered by a gang of youths in Los Angeles; and that of Red, an ancient survivor, once an oil field worker, living on the fumes of whiskey, cigarettes and an irrepressible love of life. Together these portraits form a triptych of manhood in its various ages and guises, in a gently hypnotic style that questions whether they are a product of their world or if their world is a construct of their own imagination.

**Happy People: A year in the Taiga (2010)**: A documentary depicting the life and work of the trappers of Bakhtia, a village in the heart of the Siberian Taiga, where daily life has changed little in over a century.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I feel like you would enjoy the 7up series of docs. Follows a group of children through their lives into adulthood. There’s a documentary film released that basically checks in every 7 years. Michael Apted recently died but i hope to get more of those movies while any of the subjects are still kicking!
  - The most incredible TV ever.

To see them as kids with hopes and dreams, then watch as one by one they go to uni/job centre, find work, wives/husbands, move houses, lose jobs, have mental health problems, become homeless, get divorced, (a few of them) die, decline to take part, move into age old and bad health - the whole thing is both fascinating and, yet, so melancholic that it stays with you for ages.

I think the next, and final, one is due soon.

There's only a few of the participants left now... it's real life, in the raw.
  - Wow thank you so much, sounds like my thing! ❤️
2. A lot of people might disagree but i think 'The act of killing' is deeply personal and philosophical. Its not the usual political/crime documentaries where the psychie of a hunter is explored through just talking.Here the director adopts the most absurd, most adventures way to paint a portrait of a brutal history. The result ? You will feel a very different and intense kind of disgust towards those hunter/killers and their politics while remaining totally fixated on the way how the narrative progresses.
3. Let me tell you about my three favorite documentaries of all time:     
     
1. The Devil and Daniel Johnston (2005): Explores the life and music of the oddball singer and cultural icon. You have no idea how strange and emotionally affecting his story is.  
2. Crumb (1994): Observe a true pervert (complimentary) living his best life. That's R. Crumb. Of course the real stars of the movie are his brothers, who are just as weird as him, but in wildly different ways.  
3. American Movie (1999): Mark Borchardt wants to make a movie. That's his dream, his one goal in life. The problem is, he lives in Wisconsin and has no money. What he has is a cast of friends and family who make this one of the funniest docs you'll ever see. 
  - > Crumb

One of the best documentaries ever made and best defenses of art, creativity, and community as an outlet and escape from a deeply fucked up childhood.  The whole family is so real, so hopelessly damaged.
4. 'My octopus teacher' might be a little bit different from the kind of documentaries you mentioned, but it sure has this "director's transforms to the subject" experience. Its one of most philosophical ones i watched.
5. Try these - Crumb, Surfwise, Big River Man, Project Nim, The Cruise, Pedal, Dogtown and Z-Boys, Gates of Heaven, Manda Bala,  Tony Hawk: Until the Wheels fall Off, Dig!, Beware of Mr. Baker
6. It’s not quite what you describe, because it doesn’t transpire over an especially long period of time, but “Close-Up” (1990) by Kiarostami is probably my favorite documentary ever made. It reaches this absolute empathy and humanity for the protagonist. It’s minimally editorialized and just feels like this truly authentic look at these people and casts a spotlight on this corner which easily could have come and gone without really anyone ever thinking about it, and instead the filmmakers recognized the beauty of the situation and transformed it into this wonderfully beautiful film.

“I have a message for Mahkmalbaf… Tell him that “The Cyclist” is a part of me”, this line from the film, to me, is among the most profound and beautiful statements on the impact of art and particularly film.  “Close-Up” has also become a part of me, something I look towards as inspiration during dark days.
7. "Minding The Gap" (2018) feels like exactly what you're looking for. At first glance about a group of teenage skateboarders, but becomes such a perfect encapsulation of life in midwest America.

## 2. Thaniyavarthanam (1987) is the most precise movie I have seen about superstition and societal gaslighting around it (Light spoilers below)

**Score:** 19
**Body:** ​

 Mammootty plays Balagopalan, a completely normal schoolteacher whose family has a historical pattern of the men going "mad." 

But he doesn't. He doesn't go mad. That's the point.

​What makes A.K. Lohithadas' script so devastating is how he accurately captures the mechanics of superstition. There is no grand speech where logic wins out. Instead, the belief system of the village is entirely unfalsifiable. There is no courtroom judgement. The village whispers when he is acting sane on how he is concealing it. Mammootty's performance really anchors this because it's so incredibly restrained. He isn't playing a man going crazy—he’s playing a man exhausting himself trying to be legible to people who have already made up their minds about his fate. The tragedy is entirely in that gap between his desperate effort to stay grounded and their unyielding perception of him.

​ The title translates roughly to "The Repeating Rhythm," and that's where the real thing lies. It’s not just about one man falling apart, but the realization that this was always going to happen, and will keep happening to the next man in the family, unless someone breaks the cycle. And the film leaves you with zero optimism that anyone ever will, especially when the climax hits you.

I would like to recommend this masterpiece to all the true film cinephiles. This is what you go to when you want an old movie to surprise you. It's a cult classic of the Malayalam Industry and the Malayalam Industry itself is an ocean dive for true cinema. For the movie itself I would place it among the finest psychological dramas; the plot might not be as complex as some others on the list but the scientific precision of screenplay and near-perfect molding of psych tragedy and social realism makes it unique in itself.

Thank you. It was the second time I saw this movie yesterday and I decided to post about it.

Edit: It's also exceptionally devastating... Like it won't leave you crying, and instead would leave you just emotionally disturbed and exhausted. It suffocated me psychologically and emotionally, and I believe it does that to all who watch it. It's surely among the most devastating tragedy films, and probably the only movie I have seen that traumatises you through helplessness.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. Thoughts on M BUTTERFLY (1993), directed by David Cronenberg

**Score:** 12
**Body:** I was disheartened to find out that this movie didn’t get more love when it first arrived in theatres. From a psychological perspective, there is so much going on under the hood: A French bureaucrat named Rene, who’s working and living in mainland China, is beguiled by Song Liling, a Chinese singer of Peking opera. The latter is performing the opera “Madame Butterfly” at an embassy when they first meet, and soon Rene is venturing to the local Peking opera house to see Song on stage again.

The second time around, it seems more evident that Song is actually transgender, but does that even matter to the besotted Rene? Released less than a year after Neil Jordan’s THE CRYING GAME (1992), I’m sure comparisons were inevitable between M BUTTERFLY and that other movie (If anyone reading this happened to be paying attention to cinema around this time, and can confirm how M BUTTERFLY was being covered, I’d appreciate it), but they’re actually very different, especially in their approach to the transgender aspect.

In Jordan’s film, I don’t recall it being necessarily vital to the plot (though don’t get me wrong, I still enjoyed it), whereas in Cronenberg’s it’s everything. Through Song, Rene gets to live the outdated colonialist fantasy of powerful White male savior when in his actual day job, as far as we can tell, he’s anything but that. He’s also homosexual, but since his fantasy is modeled after “Madame Butterfly”, in which a traveling sailor seduces a submissive Asian woman, Song, playing that role perfectly, allows Rene to delude himself a second time.

But Song is also a spy for the Chinese government, another complication that like the character being transgender, isn’t really treated as a twist by Cronenberg and writer David Henry Hwang (adapting his own play), which again is unlike THE CRYING GAME. On the contrary, both facts are made clear pretty much near the start, the result being that our focus is less on the plot machinations than on Rene’s behavior and what thoughts may be going through his head. As their relationship stretches across time, we may wonder how he manages not to see Song for who they are, how he can possibly play along with certain development as they arise. But given that so much of the film is glimpsed through Rene’s perspective, we also see how desperately he clings to that colonialist ideal, even as cold reality increasingly threatens to crush his illusions.

I found this to be an absolutely fascinating movie that reunites the director with much of his creative team (production designer, cinematographer, editor, composer Howard Shore) and so the end product is, of course, very well made (I especially recommend the cinematography in which the colors pop during a very Cronenberg-like finale). But it also fits seamlessly with the rest of the director’s oeuvre: Once again there’s a catalyst, be it organic or inorganic, that spurs a “mutation” or otherwise drastic shift in the main character. In the case of Song, they exist outside the standard human gender binary not unlike Genevieve Bujold’s love interest in DEAD RINGERS (1988).

In addition, there’s the director’s recurring theme of evolution that is beneficial at first, but gradually becomes detrimental, though because the timeline in M BUTTERFLY is much longer than in any of Cronenberg’s other films (But correct me if I’m wrong about that), the tragic finale, when the veil at last falls away from Rene’s eyes, hits especially hard. For me anyway, the cumulative weight of mental suffering and the sense of emotional release was up there with the ending of THE FLY (1986).

Anyway, I’d love to hear other people’s thoughts too, and I hope this film gets rediscovered and reappraised if it hasn’t already.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Several years ago, I went through Cronenberg's entire filmography over the course of a few months. *M. Butterfly* was the very last movie I watched, and even then grudgingly. I wasn't expecting much at all.

It instantly became my favorite of his films, and has remained so over multiple viewings. I think it's one of the best films of the '90s and, even more to my surprise, stunningly *Cronenbergian*. Cronenberg's films are most often about identity, and in that respect, *M. Butterfly* is right at the heart of his ouvre.

Of the many treasures, I'll share two thoughts:

* When Song first enters the courtroom during the trial scene and René sees him as a male for the first time, he smiles for a moment! In spite of the deep betrayal and unspeakably humiliating circumstances, René is happy to see him. That was an amazing choice by Jeremy Irons.
* The scene that follows in the police van is just a stone cold masterpiece of writing and acting—so much so that I forgive the total implausibility of the two of them being transported alone together.

In short: an underappreciated gem which I look forward to revisiting many more times over the years.

Whatever the case, I think it will come into its own someday. 

## 4. Irreversible (Noe) - Spoilers

**Score:** 11
**Body:** I saw Irreversible on the big screen for the first time last night. I had seen it on my laptop perhaps seven or so years prior when I was in my early twenties. I have been reading what has been said online, and in interviews Noe/the cast have given, and find that (to me) a very crucial part of the film is left out of most discussions, overshadowed (and perhaps rightfully so) by shock and/or disgust at the brutality of the scene in the tunnel.

Irreversible opens with two men lounging on the bed. One says that he has been to prison for sleeping with his daughter (he doesn't specify age, though later he does mention that she was so 'cute,' which to me implies that this was not a relationship between an adult daughter and an adult father), he appears to be free now, and the man sitting with him remarks that just because of the tragedy, the tragedy being the other man going to jail and not the abuse of the child, the joy still remains, the happy moments he had with his daughter still exist, as if time is fragmented, split off into sections that can be observed and enjoyed without truly comprehending the situation entire. 

The man also says to the other, *there are no good or bad deeds, only deeds*. 

To me, Irreversible is either arguing for or against this statement (I haven't made my mind up). Because of this statement, throughout the rest of the film we are asked to measure, to rationalize, to place each action on a plane, one side good, the other bad. It also makes us consider where the source of evil lies. 

What evil can we tolerate and what evil can we not, and why? People walk out during the scene in the tunnel and rarely before, they are able to tolerate the evil that is executed before this scene. Is it because of the stillness? Is it because culturally we view rape as a the most abhorrent act of violence? There is death within the first few minutes of film, if an audience had any real problem with depictions of violence that would be the moment they walk out. 

As we move backwards throughout the night we are asked by the film, where on the scale of good vs. bad does every action (conscious or unconscious) lie? Where does Marcus's infidelity and neglect lie? What kind of justice should be served to him? What about Pierre's objectification of Alex? Pierre's objectification of Alex has the same root as the actions that are committed in the tunnel, though he is also framed, in the first half of the film, as the one of the two men who actually loves Alex. Is it even possible to separate romantic love and sexual intimacy from objectification? 

There seems to also be the implication that perhaps the most violent, the most horrific thing that can happen to a person is being conceived and then born. The greatest crime: bringing life about. The great push out of the tunnel. To me, this is also the throbbing light at the end, the shock and pain of first seeing light as a newborn and the seeing the last light as you die. If the fate of all living things is death (time destroys all), and despite all the good one may do, or the work one may do to avoid destruction, chaos, pain, they will all surely enter one's life. Even if someone is able to live their life without ever being a victim of, or victimizing, someone else, there is natural disaster, disease, accidents. 

But to go back to *there are just deeds,* I believe that you could argue the exact opposite of what I have said in the paragraph above. That the great beauty of life is that despite great pain, misfortune, violence, life is still possible (Alex has not died, her baby could still be alive inside her). We, the audience, watching the film through, experience this exactly--after withstanding the brutality of the first half of the film we witness scenes of pleasure, beauty, humor, but all of this comes after pain.  Everywhere that there is pain there is also joy. And perhaps, the combination of the two neutralizes them, good and bad combined to make a kind of meaningless mass, that we try to parse through narrative and the assertion of the importance of our individuality. Perhaps even we (the well meaning audience) are  unable to truly witness the violence we exercise on others, just as Pierre and Marcus rationalize their violence, the Tenia rationalizes his, the man who raped his daughter rationalizes his actions as well. What do we excuse personally or culturally that inflicts pain on others? Where do our deeds lie on the scale? 

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think the reason that the rape is more often the source of shock and the breaking point of most viewers, is because there’s a kind of cinematic language we have dealing with sex and rape, a way we expect that to be softened, beyond what we normally expect from non-sexual violence in film, where we’re conditioned to see the shock coming. The rape ignores all of the cinematic shortcuts we expect (and in some ways depend on) to help create a remove between our own experience of watching and what’s happening on the screen, and *Irreversible* doesn’t give you that luxury.

It’s also a kind of violence where we’re able to watch the hurt. A murder typically involves harm, but rarely is the murdered person suffering on screen in a way we can empathize with. The rape scene in *Irreversible* is a deep-dive into prolonged torment, and that’s very difficult to endure, especially in light of the earlier paragraph.
  - even so that opening confession amp the expectation that whats coming would not be that raw

## 5. Is anyone better than PTA at ensemble casts?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Was watching Boogie Nights the other day and the thing that crept up on me, more in the days afterwards than during, was how Paul Thomas Anderson can make more characters interesting in a single film than any other director. 

I’m not saying that he understands character better than any other director, although he might, it’s that no other director consistently tells stories with that many characters that leave an impression on you. 

It’s like when you rewatch 12 Angry Men and you instantly remember how you felt about every character when it pans across the jury. But then PTA does it on the regular. 

Even supposedly great ensemble films by directors famous for them fall short. Take Casino by Scorsese. Aside from the main three characters, who can you really remember? Fat Sally… Wise Guy Jerry… some landscape gardener called Kevin? Functionally the same person three times in different positions. And that's before you get into the narration, which never stops, and is constantly explaining things you've already watched happen on screen ten seconds ago. There's a guy on the floor bleeding and De Niro's voice is calmly informing you that this is what happens when guys end up bleeding on the floor in this town. Cheers Marty. Got it. Half the runtime is someone telling you what the scene means in case you missed it, and the other half is someone telling you what's about to happen in case you were planning to be surprised. … Wait. Casino's good. Right? Casino's alright.

Sorry, back to Boogie Nights. Look at Little Bill - in a marriage that humiliates him publicly on a loop, and the way he carries it around the parties tells you everything about how long he's been absorbing it. Scotty's entire interior life is in a single carpark scene. The Colonel barely speaks and you still know exactly what kind of loneliness he's sat inside. I can think of maybe 4 other characters off the top of my head that are not “leads” and yet are given a whole existence. 

It made me wonder, do any other directors do this as well as he does?

3. Genenerally speaking,  I think PTA's real gift is the economy. He gives supporting characters just enough specificity-a gesture, an awkward conversation, a moment of humiliation or a moment of tenderness,,,,,,,that they feel like people with lives continuing off-screen.   Boogie Nights is probably his masterpiece in that regard

That said, I would put Robert Altman right alongside him. Films like Nashville and Short Cuts are almost overwhelming in how many distinct personalities they juggle 
The difference is that Altman often embraces mess and ambiguousness while PTA gives characters more  dramatic arcs

I'd also mention Akira Kurosawa in Seven Samurai, Sidney Lumet in 12 Angry Men, and even Bong Joon-ho, whose ensembles tend to make every character instantly legible without reducing them to types

But PTA may have the highest  average. In Boogie Nights, Magnolia, and even Licorice Pizza, minor characters don't merely serve the plot,,, they actually alter the emotions of the film whenever they appear. That's much rarer than it sounds 
Many directors can handle large casts:) very few make you wonder what happened to the guy who only had three scenes after the credits roll
